name: Ninazu (underworld deity)
shortDescription: Ninazu is a Sumerian deity primarily associated with the underworld, healing, and agriculture. He is often seen as a protector against demons and disease, and a guardian of life and death.
description: Ninazu is a complex Sumerian deity whose role encompasses aspects of both the underworld and the earthly realm. While primarily understood as a god of the underworld, he also holds significant connections to healing, agriculture, and protection. His name likely means 'lord of water' or 'lord of healing water', reflecting his association with subterranean waters and their life-giving properties. He is often depicted as a serpent or associated with serpents, symbols of both healing and the chthonic realm. Some texts identify him as a son of Ereshkigal, the queen of the underworld, though other genealogies vary. Ninazu's role in the underworld is not solely punitive; he is also seen as a guardian and protector, particularly against demons and diseases that originate from the netherworld. He is believed to have the power to both inflict and cure illness, further highlighting his dual nature. As an agricultural deity, Ninazu is linked to the fertility of the land and the cyclical nature of life and death, mirroring the seasons. His association with both the underworld and agriculture suggests a belief in the interconnectedness of these realms and the importance of maintaining balance between them. Ninazu's cult was centered in Enegi, a city located in southern Mesopotamia, where he was worshipped as a primary deity. While not as prominent as some other major Sumerian gods, Ninazu played a significant role in the religious and cultural beliefs of the time, embodying the essential connections between life, death, and healing.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature
sources.author: University of Oxford
